questions.specification: Which mechanism causes water to follow sodium during rehydration?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: A.2 Hydration and nutrition > A.2.1 Water and electrolyte balance > A.2.1.1 Importance of water and electrolyte balance for body functioning
labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1.1 Importance of water and electrolyte balance for body functioning
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks about the mechanism by which water follows sodium during rehydration (osmosis driven by electrolyte gradients), which is part of water and electrolyte balance.

options.content: Osmosis
options.markscheme: Correct. Sodium absorption creates an osmotic gradient, causing water to follow by osmosis into the bloodstream.

options.content: Diffusion
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Diffusion is the passive movement of solutes down their concentration gradient; it does not explain bulk water movement following sodium during rehydration.

options.content: Filtration
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Filtration refers to movement driven mainly by hydrostatic pressure (e.g. at capillaries/glomeruli), not the osmotic water movement that follows sodium absorption.

options.content: Active transport
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Active transport moves sodium (e.g. via Na$^+$/K$^+$ pumps), but the mechanism by which water follows sodium is osmosis.

parts.content: Explain how fluid replacement strategies can prevent a decline in performance in endurance events, with reference to the scenario.
parts.markscheme:
- Replacing fluid limits dehydration and associated reductions in endurance performance (e.g. earlier fatigue/increased perceived exertion) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Maintains plasma/blood volume, supporting stroke volume/cardiac output and oxygen delivery to working muscles <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supports thermoregulation by allowing continued sweating and limiting excessive rises in core temperature (particularly relevant on Day 1) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduces cardiovascular drift and helps sustain pace/power output during prolonged exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Using an appropriate drink composition (water vs electrolyte-containing drink) can help maintain sodium balance and reduce risk of hyponatremia when large volumes are consumed <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Helps maintain cognitive function/decision-making and reduces risk of cramping associated with fluid–electrolyte disturbance <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline the roles of sodium and potassium in regulating hydration status.
parts.markscheme:
- Sodium is the main extracellular cation; helps maintain extracellular osmolality and water retention <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sodium supports water absorption in the small intestine (sodium–glucose co-transport), improving fluid uptake during exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sodium promotes thirst and helps conserve water via hormonal/kidney mechanisms (e.g. ADH/aldosterone-mediated reabsorption) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Potassium is the main intracellular cation; helps maintain intracellular fluid volume/osmolality <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Potassium is important for normal nerve impulse transmission and muscle contraction; imbalance can affect fluid distribution and function <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Sodium and potassium gradients maintain osmotic balance across cell membranes and the distribution of water between compartments <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Discuss how hydration needs vary depending on environmental conditions and exercise intensity, with reference to the scenario.
parts.markscheme:
- In hot/humid conditions (Day 1), sweat rate is higher, increasing total fluid requirements to limit dehydration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher exercise intensity increases metabolic heat production and ventilation, increasing fluid loss via sweat and respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Greater sweat losses increase electrolyte (especially sodium) losses; longer/hotter sessions may require electrolyte-containing fluids rather than only plain water <marks>1 mark</marks>
- In cool conditions (Day 2), thirst can be blunted but fluid is still lost through respiration and sweat, increasing risk of under-drinking <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Individual variation (body size, fitness, sweat rate, acclimatization) means hydration strategies should be personalized <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Pre-hydration status and planned intake opportunities influence needs during the session (access to fluids, pacing, duration) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Over-drinking hypotonic fluid without replacing sodium can increase hyponatremia risk during prolonged exercise <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.content: $	ext{Na}^+$ and $	ext{K}^+$ ions are essential for transmission of action potentials in neurons.
options.markscheme: Sodium ($\text{Na}^+$) and potassium ($\text{K}^+$) ions are responsible for maintaining the resting membrane potential and generating the action potential through their movement across the neuronal membrane.

options.content: $	ext{Ca}^{2+}$ and $	ext{Mg}^{2+}$ ions affect nerve impulses mainly by controlling ATP production in neurons.
options.markscheme: While calcium ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$) is involved in neurotransmitter release and magnesium ($\text{Mg}^{2+}$) can modulate excitability at ion channels, ATP is produced through metabolic pathways (cellular respiration), not controlled directly by these electrolytes.

options.content: $	ext{Fe}^{2+}$ ions directly increase the speed of synaptic transmission by making neurotransmitters diffuse faster.
options.markscheme: Iron is involved in oxygen transport (as part of haemoglobin) and cellular respiration, but it does not directly increase synaptic transmission speed or neurotransmitter diffusion.

options.content: Electrolytes affect nerve impulses mainly by acting as the energy source that neurons use to fire.
options.markscheme: Electrolytes are ions that help set membrane potentials and excitability; they do not act as an energy source. Neurons use ATP generated by metabolic pathways such as aerobic and anaerobic respiration.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.1.2.1 Mechanisms and processes maintaining homeostasis

labeledSubSubTopics.title: A.2.1.1 Importance of water and electrolyte balance for body functioning

options.content: A decrease in the concentration of blood glucose
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Blood glucose concentration is primarily regulated by the pancreas (insulin/glucagon), not detected by hypothalamic osmoreceptors for dehydration control.

options.content: An increase in the osmolarity of the blood plasma
options.markscheme: Correct (B). Dehydration increases plasma osmolarity, detected by hypothalamic osmoreceptors, triggering thirst and increased ADH release to conserve water.

options.content: An increase in the total volume of the blood
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Dehydration tends to decrease blood/plasma volume rather than increase it.

options.content: A decrease in the partial pressure of carbon dioxide
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Changes in CO$_2$ are primarily detected by chemoreceptors involved in ventilatory control, not the hypothalamic osmoreceptor pathway for dehydration.

Participants were monitored for fluid loss and key electrolyte concentrations (Na⁺, Cl⁻, K⁺). Additionally, the table presents data for three hydration states and their associated biomarkers: plasma osmolality, urine colour score, and percentage of body mass change.

| --------------- | --------------------------- | ------------------------ | -------------------- |
| Dehydration     | 305                         | 8                        | +3.5                 |
| Euhydration     | 285                         | 4                        | +0.5                 |

Award <marks>A1</marks> for each of the following up to <marks>[2]</marks>:

parts.markscheme:

parts.content: Describe how carbohydrates are utilized differently during aerobic and anaerobic exercise.
parts.markscheme:

- in anaerobic exercise, carbohydrates are broken down without the presence of oxygen (anaerobic glycolysis) <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Explain the contribution of the anaerobic glycolytic (lactic acid) system during a $400\text{ m}$ sprint.
parts.markscheme:
- the anaerobic glycolytic system becomes the dominant energy provider after ATP-PC stores are depleted / after approximately $10\text{--}15\text{ seconds}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- it provides ATP through the incomplete breakdown of glucose (anaerobic glycolysis) <marks>A1</marks>
- this process results in the accumulation of lactic acid/lactate in the muscles and blood <marks>A1</marks>
- it supports high-intensity efforts for a duration of up to approximately $90\text{ seconds}$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Outline how balanced macronutrient intake supports sports performance.
parts.markscheme:
- carbohydrates provide the primary energy source for high-intensity training and competition <marks>A1</marks>
- proteins support muscle repair, growth, and recovery after exercise <marks>A1</marks>
- fats serve as a fuel source for low-intensity or long-duration activity <marks>A1</marks>
- a balanced intake enhances overall recovery, maintains immune function, and supports metabolic health <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Discuss the interplay of energy systems during a $1500\text{ m}$ middle-distance race.
parts.markscheme:
- all three energy systems (ATP-PC, anaerobic glycolytic, and aerobic) contribute simultaneously, shifting in their relative contribution (energy continuum) <marks>A1</marks>
- the ATP-PC system initiates the race, providing immediate energy for the first $10\text{--}15\text{ seconds}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- the anaerobic glycolytic (lactic acid) system supports sustained speed in the early stages and during any mid-race surges <marks>A1</marks>
- the aerobic system becomes the dominant provider for pacing as the race progresses and oxygen delivery meets demand <marks>A1</marks>
- toward the end of the race, the anaerobic glycolytic system contribution increases to support a final sprint finish <marks>A1</marks>

options.content: It supports nerve impulse transmission and muscle contraction by contributing to membrane depolarization.

options.content: It increases intracellular fluid volume by drawing water into cells through osmotic pressure.
